APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper fits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP venue: it tackles metonymy resolution (an established NLP subtask), introduces a novel feature-extraction method (PreWin), and releases a new dataset (ReLocaR, 1,000 train / 1,000 test). It evaluates on standard benchmarks (SemEval 2007, CoNLL subset), compares to prior SOTAs, and reports accepted metrics (accuracy, F1), so the audience and evaluation standards are appropriate. Minor concerns about the narrow subtask focus and relatively small dataset size mean it may be seen as a strong niche NLP contribution rather than a broad ML-methods paper, but it is otherwise well positioned for a top NLP conference. | B(4): The paper addresses a core NLP problem (metonymy resolution) and introduces a novel feature method (PreWin) plus a new Wikipedia-based dataset (ReLocaR), which is directly relevant to a top-tier NLP/ML audience. It uses standard methods and evaluations expected at such venues (pretrained embeddings, LSTM classifiers, accuracy/F1, comparisons to SemEval 2007 and prior SOTAs) and reports hyperparameters and related work coverage. Minor concerns for a top-tier venue are the relatively small training sizes (~1,000 examples per dataset) and modest SOTA improvement on SemEval (86.3% vs 86.2%), which may raise questions about broader impact and scale.

It evaluates on standard benchmarks (SemEval 2007, CoNLL subset), compares to prior SOTAs, and reports accepted metrics (accuracy, F1), so the audience and evaluation standards are appropriate. Minor concerns about the narrow subtask focus and relatively small dataset size mean it may be seen as a strong niche NLP contribution rather than a broad ML-methods paper, but it is otherwise well positioned for a top NLP conference.

Minor concerns for a top-tier venue are the relatively small training sizes (~1,000 examples per dataset) and modest SOTA improvement on SemEval (86.3% vs 86.2%), which may raise questions about broader impact and scale.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents a modestly novel feature-extraction heuristic (PreWin) that leverages dependency head relations to select a small predicate-context window, and it contributes a new, more balanced Wikipedia-derived dataset (ReLocaR). These elements are clear differentiators from prior sliding-window or feature-rich systems, but the core modeling (LSTM with pretrained embeddings) and the gains are incremental (small SOTA improvement and similar resources). Overall this reads as a practical, targeted innovation rather than a substantial methodological breakthrough that would surprise experts. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new, focused feature-extraction method (PreWin) that leverages dependency head relations to select up to five contextual words, which is a specific methodological innovation beyond standard window or parse-pruning approaches. It also contributes a new, more balanced Wikipedia-based dataset (ReLocaR) and demonstrates that a minimalist LSTM using PreWin can match or slightly exceed prior SOTA (ensemble 86.3% vs previous 86.2%), differentiating its contribution from feature-rich prior systems. The techniques are a non-trivial, practical extension rather than a radical new paradigm, so the novelty is notable but somewhat incremental given the modest performance gain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a modestly novel feature-extraction heuristic (PreWin) that leverages dependency head relations to select a small predicate-context window, and it contributes a new, more balanced Wikipedia-derived dataset (ReLocaR). CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized (clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (PreWin, the ReLocaR dataset, and SOTA results). Notation and methodological description are consistent and complete enough to report hyperparameters and reproduce the LSTM/embedding setup, and related work is covered thoroughly. Figures/tables are not detailed in the summary but experimental comparisons (baselines, metrics) appear clearly presented; overall writing quality is described as good with only minor presentation limitations. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (PreWin, ReLocaR dataset, SOTA results). The method is clearly described (SpaCy dependency-based Predicate Window, LSTM classifier, embeddings) and experiments/datasets/hyperparameters are reported, with limitations and related work discussed. Minor presentation issues remain (the summary does not confirm the effectiveness/clarity of figures/tables or detailed notation examples), so I rate clarity as very good but not flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized (clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (PreWin, the ReLocaR dataset, and SOTA results). MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against a wide range of baselines (immediate 5/10-word windows, paragraph baseline, extreme parse-tree pruning) and prior SOTA systems (e.g., Nastase & Strube, Farkas et al., Brun et al.) on the same benchmarks (SemEval 2007, CoNLL subset) using standard metrics (accuracy, F1). Comparisons appear fair (same data and evaluation metrics), the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (reporting 86.3% vs prior 86.2%) and analyze differences and failure modes of PreWin (context loss, parsing errors, data limits). Related work coverage is described as thorough and relevant competing approaches are cited and discussed. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate baselines (immediate/paragraph windows, parse-pruning) and prior SOTA systems (e.g., Nastase & Strube, Farkas et al., Brun et al.), reports on the same standard datasets (SemEval 2007, CoNLL subset) and uses common metrics (accuracy, F1). Comparisons appear fair and conditions comparable, and the authors explicitly note when prior methods are comparable (e.g., 86.3% vs 86.2% SOTA) and discuss failure cases and differences between PreWin and baselines. Related work coverage is described as thorough and competing approaches are cited and analyzed.

Comparisons appear fair (same data and evaluation metrics), the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (reporting 86.3% vs prior 86.2%) and analyze differences and failure modes of PreWin (context loss, parsing errors, data limits). SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are generally supported by experiments: a novel PreWin feature is evaluated against appropriate baselines (immediate windows, paragraph, parse-pruning) on SemEval 2007 and the newly constructed ReLocaR dataset, hyperparameters are reported, and per-class metrics are given. However, the reported SOTA improvement is very small (86.3% vs 86.2%) and the summary does not indicate statistical significance testing, so that claim may not be robust. There are reasonable caveats acknowledged (parser errors, limited ~1k training examples, overfitting with larger embeddings), which limit generality but do not invalidate the experimental results. Overall the methodology is sound with minor issues (lack of significance analysis and modest dataset size) that do not overturn the paper's conclusions. | B(4): The main claims are generally supported: experiments report state-of-the-art accuracy on SemEval (86.3% vs prior 86.2%) and strong results on the newly introduced ReLocaR dataset, and the paper compares against sensible baselines and reports hyperparameters. No mathematical derivations are required and none are problematic. However, the methodology lacks mention of statistical significance testing and relies on a small (~1,000-sentence) dataset and an ensemble, raising concerns about robustness and potential overfitting; dependence on parser/tokenizer quality is another acknowledged limitation. Overall the technical evidence is sound but has minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are generally supported by experiments: a novel PreWin feature is evaluated against appropriate baselines (immediate windows, paragraph, parse-pruning) on SemEval 2007 and the newly constructed ReLocaR dataset, hyperparameters are reported, and per-class metrics are given. However, the reported SOTA improvement is very small (86.3% vs 86.2%) and the summary does not indicate statistical significance testing, so that claim may not be robust. There are reasonable caveats acknowledged (parser errors, limited ~1k training examples, overfitting with larger embeddings), which limit generality but do not invalidate the experimental results. Overall the methodology is sound with minor issues (lack of significance analysis and modest dataset size) that do not overturn the paper's conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are solid: the paper evaluates PreWin against multiple strong baselines (immediate/paragraph windows, extreme parse pruning) and prior SOTAs across SemEval 2007 and the new ReLocaR dataset, and reports hyperparameters. Appropriate sensitivity analyses are present (embedding dimensionality, single models vs ensemble) and the paper discusses failure cases and limitations (lossy predicate paths, parsing/tokenization errors, limited training data). Related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor reproducibility concerns remain (code not mentioned and the new dataset is relatively small), so I deduct one point for those gaps. | B(4): The paper presents strong empirical support: it introduces and releases the ReLocaR dataset, evaluates on SemEval and CoNLL (reporting a new SOTA), and compares PreWin against multiple baselines (immediate windows, paragraph, parse pruning). Ablations/sensitivity checks are present (embedding dimensionality, different window baselines) and hyperparameters are reported; failure cases and limitations are explicitly discussed. Related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor gaps remain (code availability is not mentioned and some finer-grained ablations—e.g., detailed analysis of ensemble vs single-model gains or wider hyperparameter sweeps—are not fully explored), so the experiments are solid but not exhaustively comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are solid: the paper evaluates PreWin against multiple strong baselines (immediate/paragraph windows, extreme parse pruning) and prior SOTAs across SemEval 2007 and the new ReLocaR dataset, and reports hyperparameters. Appropriate sensitivity analyses are present (embedding dimensionality, single models vs ensemble) and the paper discusses failure cases and limitations (lossy predicate paths, parsing/tokenization errors, limited training data). Related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor reproducibility concerns remain (code not mentioned and the new dataset is relatively small), so I deduct one point for those gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents strong empirical support: it introduces and releases the ReLocaR dataset, evaluates on SemEval and CoNLL (reporting a new SOTA), and compares PreWin against multiple baselines (immediate windows, paragraph, parse pruning). Ablations/sensitivity checks are present (embedding dimensionality, different window baselines) and hyperparameters are reported; failure cases and limitations are explicitly discussed. Related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor gaps remain (code availability is not mentioned and some finer-grained ablations—e.g., detailed analysis of ensemble vs single-model gains or wider hyperparameter sweeps—are not fully explored), so the experiments are solid but not exhaustively comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles the focused but meaningful problem of metonymy resolution and contributes a novel, lightweight PreWin dependency-based context extractor and a new balanced Wikipedia-derived dataset (ReLocaR, 1,000 train / 1,000 test). The approach yields a marginal new SOTA (86.3% on SemEval 2007) and shows that a minimalist LSTM with small pretrained embeddings can beat prior feature-rich systems, which could shift some engineering practices. However, the gains are incremental, the method has known limitations (discarding distant context, small training sizes), and its applicability is mainly within the metonymy/NER subfield rather than transformative across NLP. | B(3): The paper targets the important task of metonymy resolution and contributes a simple, interpretable feature-extraction method (PreWin) plus a new balanced dataset (ReLocaR), which are likely to be useful to researchers. Empirically their minimalist LSTM with PreWin matches or slightly improves prior SOTA (86.3% vs 86.2% on SemEval) and achieves strong results on ReLocaR (85.7%), showing the value of dependency-based context selection without heavy resources. However, the empirical gains are modest, and noted limitations (small training sizes, parser/tokenization errors, and possible loss of distant context) constrain transformative impact, so the contribution is of moderate importance rather than field-changing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the focused but meaningful problem of metonymy resolution and contributes a novel, lightweight PreWin dependency-based context extractor and a new balanced Wikipedia-derived dataset (ReLocaR, 1,000 train / 1,000 test). The approach yields a marginal new SOTA (86.3% on SemEval 2007) and shows that a minimalist LSTM with small pretrained embeddings can beat prior feature-rich systems, which could shift some engineering practices. However, the gains are incremental, the method has known limitations (discarding distant context, small training sizes), and its applicability is mainly within the metonymy/NER subfield rather than transformative across NLP.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper targets the important task of metonymy resolution and contributes a simple, interpretable feature-extraction method (PreWin) plus a new balanced dataset (ReLocaR), which are likely to be useful to researchers. Empirically their minimalist LSTM with PreWin matches or slightly improves prior SOTA (86.3% vs 86.2% on SemEval) and achieves strong results on ReLocaR (85.7%), showing the value of dependency-based context selection without heavy resources. However, the empirical gains are modest, and noted limitations (small training sizes, parser/tokenization errors, and possible loss of distant context) constrain transformative impact, so the contribution is of moderate importance rather than field-changing.

main_claims: Introduce PreWin, a novel Predicate Window feature-extraction method using dependency head relations that selects up to 5 words of context (excluding the entity) to improve metonymy resolution.

Create and release a new Wikipedia-based dataset called ReLocaR for metonymy resolution (1,000 training and 1,000 test instances) with a more balanced class distribution and revised annotation scheme.
Achieve state-of-the-art performance: an ensemble reached 86.3% accuracy on the SemEval 2007 dataset (previous SOTA 86.2%) and 85.7% accuracy on ReLocaR.
Demonstrate that a minimalist neural approach (LSTM with modest resources and no external lexical resources) combined with PreWin can outperform prior feature-rich systems.
_summary.methodology: Use SpaCy dependency parsing to locate the entity head and extract a Predicate Window of up to 5 non-punctuation, non-conjunct words away from the entity head as input (entity text omitted); represent words with pretrained embeddings (default 50-dim) and classify with an LSTM (Keras), optionally combining multiple models in an ensemble.
baselines: Extreme parse tree pruning (short input via long-range dependencies)
Previous SOTA systems (e.g., Nastase & Strube 2009/2013, Farkas et al. 2007, Brun et al. 2007)
limitations: PreWin can discard important context (e.g., when relevant words lie beyond the chosen predicate path/right-hand side)
Fails on more complex semantic predicates and uncommon constructions due to limited training data (~1,000 sentences per dataset)
Parsing and tokenization errors (e.g., acronyms, hyphenation) can degrade performance
Larger embedding dimensionalities (100, 300) led to overfitting and decreased accuracy in their experiments
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Metonymy resolution / Named Entity Recognition
